title: Database Modeling and MySQL DDL Review Quiz
subtitle: ER modeling, crow foot notation, constraints, and table design

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the Entity-Relationship (ER) model in database design?
options: To describe data structure and relationships conceptually
To replace the need for foreign keys
To store data in JSON format
questions.explanation: The ER model is used for conceptual database design to represent entities, attributes, and relationships.

questions.question: Which notation is commonly used to represent ER relationships in practice?
questions.explanation: Crow Foot notation is widely used to represent cardinalities and relationships visually.

questions.question: In ER modeling, what best describes an entity?
options: An object or concept such as Student or Course
questions.explanation: Entities represent real-world objects or concepts that are stored as tables.

questions.question: What is an attribute in ER modeling?
questions.explanation: Attributes are the descriptive properties of entities, such as name or address.

questions.question: What does cardinality describe in a relationship?
options: The physical storage size of a table
The number of instances participating in the relationship
The number of indexes on a table
The order of columns in a schema
questions.explanation: Cardinality indicates how many entities can be related, such as one-to-many or many-to-many.

questions.question: Which of the following is a one-to-many relationship?
options: One student enrolls in one course only
Many students belong to many departments
One bill is linked to many bills
questions.explanation: One doctor can schedule many appointments, while each appointment belongs to one doctor.

questions.question: In crow foot notation, what does a circle typically indicate?
questions.explanation: A circle indicates that participation is optional, meaning zero is allowed.

questions.question: In crow foot notation, what does a crow's foot symbol usually represent?
questions.explanation: The crow's foot symbol represents the 'many' side of a relationship.

questions.question: What relationship type usually requires a junction table?
questions.explanation: Many-to-many relationships are typically implemented with a junction table.

questions.question: Why is the Entity-Relationship model useful before writing SQL tables?
options: It avoids the need for constraints
It helps identify entities, relationships, and keys before implementation
It removes the need for normalization
questions.explanation: ER modeling supports clear logical design before implementing physical tables.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a primary key?
options: A column used only for sorting
A column or set of columns that uniquely identifies a row
A column that may contain duplicate values
A column that links to another table but does not need uniqueness
questions.explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each row and prevents duplicates.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a foreign key?
options: To ensure column values are unique
To reference a row in another table and preserve integrity
questions.explanation: A foreign key links to a referenced table and enforces referential integrity.

questions.question: Which SQL statement is used to add a new column to an existing table?
questions.explanation: In MySQL, ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN is used to modify the table structure.

questions.question: Which SQL statement removes a column from an existing table in MySQL?
questions.explanation: MySQL uses ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN to remove a column.

questions.question: What does ON DELETE SET NULL do in a foreign key constraint?
options: Sets the foreign key column to NULL in child rows
Copies the parent key into the child row
questions.explanation: If the parent row is deleted, the referencing foreign key becomes NULL when SET NULL is used.

questions.question: What does ON UPDATE CASCADE do in a foreign key relationship?
options: Deletes child rows when the parent changes
Updates child key values automatically when the parent key changes
Prevents any update in the parent table
Sets child key values to NULL
questions.explanation: CASCADE propagates changes in the referenced key to the child table.

questions.question: Why are primary keys important in relational databases?
options: They ensure row uniqueness and support reliable updates and references
They replace the need for indexes
questions.explanation: Primary keys uniquely identify tuples and help maintain data integrity.

questions.question: Which MySQL storage engine is recommended for most transactional databases?
questions.explanation: InnoDB supports ACID transactions and foreign keys, making it the standard choice.

questions.question: Which storage engine stores data in RAM?
questions.explanation: The MEMORY engine stores table data in memory for temporary or fast-access use cases.

questions.question: Which storage engine is described as having table-level locking and fast reads?
questions.explanation: MyISAM is known for table-level locking and being read-oriented.

questions.question: What is the best reason to avoid storing calculated totals as the only source of truth?
options: They can become inconsistent if source data changes
They require too many foreign keys
They are not compatible with SQL
questions.explanation: Derived values should usually be computed from raw data to avoid inconsistency.

questions.question: Why is storing comma-separated values in one column usually a bad design choice?
options: It uses too much disk space only
It breaks atomicity and makes querying harder
questions.explanation: Comma-separated lists violate normalization and make searching and updating difficult.

questions.question: Which design choice is preferred for a user identifier in most systems?
options: A meaningful natural key like email
A surrogate key like auto-increment user_id
A calculated hash of name and birthdate
A text field with duplicate values allowed
questions.explanation: Surrogate keys are stable even if business data such as email changes.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using NOT NULL on important columns?
questions.explanation: NOT NULL ensures required data must be provided for the column.

questions.question: Which of the following is most likely a many-to-many relationship in an e-commerce system?
questions.explanation: A product can belong to many categories and a category can contain many products.

questions.question: Which table best represents the relationship between Products and Categories?
questions.explanation: A junction table is needed to implement the many-to-many relation.

questions.question: In a self-referencing foreign key, what is the referenced row usually in the same table?
options: A row in the same table
questions.explanation: A self-referencing foreign key points back to another row in the same table.

questions.question: Which entity in the appointment system generates exactly one bill?
questions.explanation: The relationship states that each appointment generates exactly one bill.

questions.question: How many doctors can be linked to one appointment in the appointment system?
questions.explanation: Each appointment is scheduled with exactly one doctor.

questions.question: How many appointments can one patient schedule according to the exercise description?
questions.explanation: The patient can schedule one or more appointments.

questions.question: What does the statement 'a bill can be outstanding' imply?
options: The bill must already be fully paid
The bill may have no payments yet
The bill cannot be linked to payments
The bill must be deleted after creation
questions.explanation: An outstanding bill means there may be nothing paid on it yet.

questions.question: Which referential action is best if a doctor may be deleted but appointments should remain?
questions.explanation: SET NULL preserves the appointment while removing the reference to the deleted doctor.

questions.question: Which SQL clause belongs in a CREATE TABLE statement to choose the storage engine?
questions.explanation: MySQL uses ENGINE=InnoDB to specify the storage engine.

questions.question: What is the main goal of normalization?
options: To reduce redundancy and improve consistency
To make every table have only one column
questions.explanation: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.question: What is the first step in the database design process listed in the notes?
questions.explanation: Database design starts by identifying what data needs to be stored.

questions.question: Which step converts an ER diagram into table schemas?
questions.explanation: Logical design transforms the conceptual model into relations and schemas.

questions.question: What is the role of physical design in the database design process?
options: Selecting data types and storage details
questions.explanation: Physical design chooses data types, storage, and implementation details.

questions.question: Which of the following is a typical advantage of InnoDB over MyISAM?
options: ACID compliance and foreign key support
questions.explanation: InnoDB is transactional and supports foreign keys and row-level locking.

questions.question: What is the best reason to use a junction table in relational design?
options: To convert a many-to-many relationship into two one-to-many relationships
To remove the need for keys
questions.explanation: A junction table resolves many-to-many relationships into implementable relations.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a UNIQUE constraint?
options: It allows duplicate values but no NULLs
It guarantees uniqueness of a column or column set
It creates a foreign key automatically
It deletes rows with duplicate values
questions.explanation: A UNIQUE constraint prevents duplicate values in the constrained column(s).

questions.question: Why can email be a poor choice as a primary key in a user table?
questions.explanation: Primary keys should be stable, and email addresses can change.

questions.question: Which relationship is typically implemented with a foreign key in the 'many' table?
options: Many-to-many directly without a junction table
questions.explanation: In one-to-many relationships, the foreign key usually resides in the many-side table.

questions.question: What happens if a foreign key references a deleted row and no referential action allows it?
options: The key becomes a primary key
questions.explanation: Foreign key references must remain valid to preserve referential integrity.

questions.question: Which column is most likely to be a foreign key in the Orders table of an e-commerce system?
questions.explanation: customer_id links Orders to Customers and is therefore a foreign key.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best reason to use CHECK constraints?
options: To enforce domain rules such as valid price or status values
questions.explanation: CHECK constraints enforce valid values according to business rules.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a junction table in an online bookstore?
questions.explanation: Book_Authors connects books and authors in a many-to-many relationship.

questions.question: Why is an order item table needed in an order system?
options: To represent which products belong to each order and in what quantity
questions.explanation: Order_Items stores the line-level details of products in each order.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a many-to-many relationship between students and sections?
options: Each student can enroll in many sections and each section can have many students
Each student can enroll in only one section
Each section belongs to exactly one student
Each student must have one section and one section only
questions.explanation: Many students can enroll in many sections, requiring a junction table.

questions.question: What is the main risk of over-normalization?
options: Excessive joins and reduced query performance
questions.explanation: Over-normalization can make queries complex and slower due to many joins.

questions.question: Which statement is most accurate about designing a social media database?
options: Likes and follows should always be stored as text fields
Users, posts, comments, and hashtags are likely separate entities with relationships
Only one table is needed for all social media data
questions.explanation: Social media systems are naturally modeled with multiple entities and relationships.

questions.question: When a patient is insured, what does the model say about their insurance company relationship?
options: A patient can carry insurance with multiple companies simultaneously
A patient can only carry insurance with one company
Insurance companies cannot have many patients
questions.explanation: The exercise states that an insured patient can only carry insurance with one company.

questions.question: Which SQL command family is used for schema changes such as adding or removing columns?
questions.explanation: DDL statements modify the structure of database objects.

questions.question: What does the term referential integrity primarily protect against?
questions.explanation: Referential integrity ensures that foreign key references always point to valid rows.

questions.question: Which of the following best represents a relationship where a course belongs to exactly one section?
options: Optional many-to-one from course to section
questions.explanation: If each course belongs to exactly one section and each section maps to one course, it is one-to-one.

questions.question: Why might an appointment date alone be insufficient as a primary key in the appointment system?
options: Dates cannot be stored in MySQL
More than one appointment can occur on the same date
questions.explanation: A date alone is not guaranteed to be unique because many appointments can share the same date.

questions.question: What is the best implementation choice for a product-category many-to-many relation?
options: Store category names as comma-separated text in Products
Create a Product_Categories table with foreign keys to both tables
Duplicate product rows for each category
Use a trigger instead of a table
questions.explanation: A junction table is the standard relational implementation for many-to-many relationships.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why tables should have clearly defined primary keys before adding foreign keys?
options: Foreign keys do not work without auto-increment
Referenced rows must be uniquely identifiable
Foreign keys can reference any random column
questions.explanation: Foreign keys reference primary or candidate keys so the target row is unique.

questions.question: Which referential rule is most appropriate when deleting a product that is already part of historical order items?
options: SET NULL on every order item without exception
questions.explanation: RESTRICT prevents deletion when dependent historical data must be preserved.

questions.question: What is the strongest justification for using ON DELETE CASCADE on a child table?
options: You want child rows removed automatically when the parent is removed
You want to preserve orphaned child rows
You want to convert a foreign key into a primary key
You want to allow duplicate parent rows
questions.explanation: CASCADE is appropriate when child rows should not exist without the parent.

questions.question: Which stage of database design is most closely associated with creating the actual database objects in SQL?
questions.explanation: Implementation is where the database is actually created using SQL.

questions.question: What does the phrase 'good design upfront saves hours of refactoring later' emphasize?
options: Database design is unnecessary if the project is small
Early modeling reduces later structural changes and errors
Constraints should be added only after deployment
questions.explanation: Careful early design helps avoid expensive schema corrections later.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid reason to use DEFAULT in a table definition?
options: To provide a value when none is supplied
To remove NULL values from all existing rows instantly
questions.explanation: DEFAULT supplies a fallback value when no explicit value is inserted.

questions.question: Which relationship is most likely one-to-many in a university database?
options: Professors to Office Rooms if each office has many professors
Courses to Departments if each course belongs to one department
questions.explanation: A course usually belongs to one department, while a department can have many courses.

questions.question: What is the correct interpretation of a patient making many payments but a single payment belonging to only one patient?
questions.explanation: One patient can create many payments, so the relationship is one-to-many.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of a relational anti-pattern mentioned in the notes?
options: Storing image_url instead of a BLOB
Adding a foreign key to a junction table
questions.explanation: Using VARCHAR(255) for every column is called out as lazy design.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best answer when asked to draw a conceptual model for a bookstore?
options: List only tables and ignore relationships
Identify entities, attributes, primary keys, and relationships
Use only one giant table for all data
questions.explanation: Conceptual modeling focuses on entities, attributes, keys, and relationships.
